addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer

 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries

 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

1	# Versioning
2	
3	How this project versions its commits and the running
4	artifact. The scheme — the cycle step-numbering and the
5	unique-per-commit aim — is generic and shared across projects:
6	this file is copied **verbatim**, with
7	[Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
8	covering each medium by conditional rather than per-project
9	edits.
10	
11	## Terms
12	
13	Three names, used as defined here across
14	[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md),
15	[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md), and the notes files:
16	
17	- **version** — the per-commit version (e.g. `0.3.0-5.3.0`),
18	  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
19	  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
20	  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).
21	- **version-of-record** — the authoritative stored copy of the
22	  version, in the project's manifest (see
23	  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
24	  a built or running artifact derives from it.
25	- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.
26	
27	## Recording the version-of-record
28	
29	Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
30	surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
31	your medium:
32	
33	- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
34	  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
35	  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
36	    committed config it's sourced from)
37	  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
38	    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
39	    needed
40	- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
41	  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
42	    store it verbatim
43	  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
44	    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
45	    version, just the stored spelling
46	- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
47	  version-of-record:
48	  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
49	  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
50	- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see
51	  [Unique per commit](#unique-per-commit-preference-not-requirement);
52	  this project follows the per-commit preference.
53	
54	## Unique per commit (preference, not requirement)
55	
56	Our general notion is that the version-of-record should change
57	on **every commit**, so a built or running artifact identifies
58	the exact commit it came from.
59	
60	- This is a preference, **not** a hard requirement. A project
61	  following the cycle protocol may bump less often — once per
62	  cycle, only at release, and so on. Record the choice in
63	  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
64	  if it differs.
65	- It is achievable because the cycle's versions (below) are
66	  **pre-assignable**, unlike the git SHA, which a commit cannot
67	  contain (see the cycle protocol's
68	  [Commits backfill](cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill)).
69	
70	## Step numbering
71	
72	The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
73	[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
74	phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
75	a Preparation**:
76	
77	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
78	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
79	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
80	
81	**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
82	no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
83	Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
84	needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
85	grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
86	renumbering siblings.
87	
88	Disambiguation:
89	
90	- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
91	  Preparation.
92	- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
93	  identifier `0`).
94	
95	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
96	
97	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
98	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
99	- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
100	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
101	
102	Bump the version-of-record at the start of each phase, so the
103	active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,
104	every commit carries a distinct version.
105

# Versioning

How this project versions its commits and the running
artifact. The scheme — the cycle step-numbering and the
unique-per-commit aim — is generic and shared across projects:
this file is copied **verbatim**, with
[Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
covering each medium by conditional rather than per-project
edits.

## Terms

Three names, used as defined here across
[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md),
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md), and the notes files:

- **version** — the per-commit version (e.g. `0.3.0-5.3.0`),
  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).
- **version-of-record** — the authoritative stored copy of the
  version, in the project's manifest (see
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
  a built or running artifact derives from it.
- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.

## Recording the version-of-record

Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
your medium:

- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
    committed config it's sourced from)
  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
    needed
- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
    store it verbatim
  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
    version, just the stored spelling
- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
  version-of-record:
  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see
  [Unique per commit](#unique-per-commit-preference-not-requirement);
  this project follows the per-commit preference.

## Unique per commit (preference, not requirement)

Our general notion is that the version-of-record should change
on **every commit**, so a built or running artifact identifies
the exact commit it came from.

- This is a preference, **not** a hard requirement. A project
  following the cycle protocol may bump less often — once per
  cycle, only at release, and so on. Record the choice in
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
  if it differs.
- It is achievable because the cycle's versions (below) are
  **pre-assignable**, unlike the git SHA, which a commit cannot
  contain (see the cycle protocol's
  [Commits backfill](cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill)).

## Step numbering

The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
renumbering siblings.

Disambiguation:

- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Bump the version-of-record at the start of each phase, so the
active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,
every commit carries a distinct version.

1	# bdimager
2	
3	When complete this Python tool will allow you to take a working
4	image from a block device, typically an sd-card, and
5	- Capture the contents to a file *mother* image
6	- Build the this into distributable(?) image
7	- Write the distributable image to a target block device
8	  and this target device can be a difference size than
9	  the *mother* image.
10	
11	Eventually I'd like to convert this to Rust.
12	
13	## Repo
14	
15	This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
16	a bot to help develop a project — code, but equally prose, an
17	image, a song, a screenplay, anything the bot generates from a
18	conversation. The goal is that this main repo contains the
19	"what" (the artifact), while the partner bot repo contains the
20	"why" and "how" (the conversation). The key to the convention is
21	each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there is a
22	coherent story of the development of the project across time.
23	
24	The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
25	which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
26	first test bed.
27	
28	### Assumptions
29	
30	- **The [`vc-x1`](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1) companion tool**
31	  will be used to help manage the repo the underlying jj steps are
32	  documented so the flow also works without it.
33	
34	### Cloning
35	
36	`vc-x1 clone ..` can be used to clone
37	
38	## Releasing
39	
40	TBD
41	
42	## jj Tips for Git Users
43	
44	This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
45	alongside git. New to jj? See
46	[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
47	[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).
48	
49	Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
50	force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
51	live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).
52	
53	## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers
54	
55	Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
56	to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
57	`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
58	dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
59	the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
60	see
61	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).
62	
63	## Contributing
64	
65	Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
66	canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
67	and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):
68	
69	- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
70	  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
71	  Close-out).
72	- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)

# bdimager

When complete this Python tool will allow you to take a working
image from a block device, typically an sd-card, and
- Capture the contents to a file *mother* image
- Build the this into distributable(?) image
- Write the distributable image to a target block device
  and this target device can be a difference size than
  the *mother* image.

Eventually I'd like to convert this to Rust.

## Repo

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop a project — code, but equally prose, an
image, a song, a screenplay, anything the bot generates from a
conversation. The goal is that this main repo contains the
"what" (the artifact), while the partner bot repo contains the
"why" and "how" (the conversation). The key to the convention is
each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there is a
coherent story of the development of the project across time.

The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
first test bed.

### Assumptions

- **The [`vc-x1`](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1) companion tool**
  will be used to help manage the repo the underlying jj steps are
  documented so the flow also works without it.

### Cloning

`vc-x1 clone ..` can be used to clone

TBD

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)

  Close-out).

  shape.

content.text:
Reviewed both. Here's what I found.

## README.md

**Typos / grammar**
- Line 4: "Build the **this** into distributable(?)" → "Build **this** into a distributable…" (and the `(?)` placeholder needs resolving).
- Line 8: "a **difference** size" → "a **different** size".
- Line 36: "`vc-x1 clone ..` can be used to clone" — sentence trails off mid-thought (clone *what*, to *where*?). The `## Releasing` section is also a bare "TBD". Both are obvious placeholders.

**Substantive: Rust scaffolding vs. a Python project**
This is the one worth a decision. Line 3 states bdimager is a **Python** tool ("Eventually I'd like to convert this to Rust"). But the inherited `Contributing` section points at Rust/cargo machinery:
- Line 75–77: "Per-commit flow — the **cargo cycle** plus the Work and Commit-Description review gates."
- Line 78–80: "Code Conventions — doc comments on every **file / fn / method**, `// OK: …` on **`unwrap*`** calls…"

`unwrap*` and `.rs` doc comments don't apply to Python. AGENTS.md already anticipates medium-specificity ("the Rust example is the cargo cycle; skip-able for notes-only"), so the README's Contributing bullets are just over-specific to Rust. Either soften them to medium-neutral wording now, or defer until the Python code lands and adapt the conventions then.

**Minor**
- Lines 24–27: "The beginnings of that tool is vc-x1 … but is being used as a first test bed." The referent of "is being used as a test bed" is ambiguous (vc-x1? bdimager?).

## notes/todo.md

- **`## Todo` shape doesn't match the convention.** The format expects priority-ranked entries, each a standalone task titled by its name. What's there is a single "Initial Plan:" with a 1–4 numbered list — those are *sequential steps of one cycle*, not four ranked todos. Fine as a temporary stand-in, but when you pick it up it should become an `## In Progress` ladder (`0.3.0-0`, `-1`, …), not stay as Todo numbers.
- **`## Done` and `# References` are now empty.** They held two entries earlier this session (`docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)`, `docs: versioning SSOT…` with `[1]`/`[3]` refs) — they've since been cleared. If that was intentional (a reset), fine; flagging in case it wasn't.
- **Version jump 0.1.0 → 0.3.0.** Step 4 targets close-out of 0.3.0, but the last recorded Done was 0.1.0. If 0.2.0 was the scaffold cycle that's consistent; otherwise the target may want to be 0.2.0.
- **`versioning.md` doesn't yet name bdimager's manifest.** Step 4 ("close out 0.3.0") needs a version-of-record location, but no `Cargo.toml`/`pyproject.toml` exists. For a Python tool that'd be `pyproject.toml [project].version` with the `-`→`+` remap noted in versioning.md.

Want me to fix the README typos and the Rust-vs-Python wording, or leave this as a review only?

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
is the active one in this template), and earlier ones are closed and
kept for history.


Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):

- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
  Done entries.
- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
  rules, `Commits:` line format.
- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.

Per-cycle workflow lives in
[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
  sub-cycles.
- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
  work + commit description review gates.
- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix; body shape
  per app vs `.claude` repo.
- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.

This repo's versioning lives in
[`versioning.md`](versioning.md) — generic and shared
verbatim; the single source of truth that AGENTS.md and
cycle-protocol.md refer to abstractly:

- [Terms](versioning.md#terms) — version / version-of-record /
  versioning.
- [Recording the version-of-record](versioning.md#recording-the-version-of-record)
  — manifest, notation, reporter, and cadence, by medium.
- [Step numbering](versioning.md#step-numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` scheme, nesting, optional Preparation.

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)
  and drops the `(<version>)` title suffix.

1	# Jujutsu (jj) Tips
2	
3	See [Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)
4	[Jujutsu-tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial)
5	and [jj docs](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/).
6	
7	## Initial Commit for a repo
8	
9	Create a directory and add your files.
10	
11	Minimal commands to push
12	
13	```
14	jj git init .
15	jj describe
16	jj git remote add origin git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
17	jj bookmark create main -r @
18	jj bookmark track main --remote=origin
19	jj git push
20	```
21	
22	## Push a change to main
23	
24	Assuming that this is to be push to main you
25	set the bookmark to the appropriate commit and
26	then just push:
27	
28	```
29	jj bookmark set main -r @
30	jj git push
31	```
32	
33	Complete example:
34	```
35	wink@3900x 26-03-13T17:26:21.177Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1 ((jj/keep/1a79f803025f75fb557a7b6f9d29e3dbee6a1724))
36	$ vi README.md

157	## Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`
158	
159	If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
160	tools like `gitk --all`.
161	
162	jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
163	When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
164	jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
165	undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
166	shows the current version of each change.
167	
168	## Useful commands
169	
170	| Command | Description |
171	|---------|-------------|
172	| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
173	| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
174	| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
175	| `jj st` | Show the status of the Working and Parent commits |
176	| `jj st -r <chid>` | Status of the commit, `<chid>` such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
177	| `jj show` | Show the Working commit (`-r @`) |
178	| `jj show -r <chid>` | Show the commit, `<chid>` such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
179	| `jj evolog -r <chid>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
180	| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |
181	
182	
183	In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
184	the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.
185	
186	## Revsets
187	
188	Revsets (sets of revisions) are how jj addresses commits. A
189	revision is identified by `@`, a chid, or a cid, and revsets are
190	built from those identifiers plus the operators `-`, `+`, `..`,
191	and `::`. There is a complete language available — see
192	`jj help -k revsets`. The [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh)
193	script scaffolds the example repo used below.
194	
195	Summary:
196	
197	- A revision (rev) is used for addressing/referring to commits.
198	- jj supports a change_id (chid) and commit_id (cid).
199	  - The chid is permanent and doesn't change.
200	  - The cid is the SHA of the current commit and changes with any
201	    change to it or its ancestors.
202	- A revset is a set of revisions.
203	- In many jj commands revsets can address multiple commits.
204	- There is one root commit; chid=zzz sha=0 owner=root.
205	- The root commit never has content nor description.
206	- There can be multiple independent lineages of commits off root():
207	  - to create:
208	    - `jj new 'root()'`
209	    - `git switch --orphan <name>`
210	  - to see these:
211	    - `jj log -r 'roots(~root())'`
212	    - `git log --all --max-parents=0 --oneline`
213	
214	The examples below show these primitives in action.
215	
216	### A jj repo with some commits
217	
218	Some examples of revisions/rev:
219	   - @, chid=tkpvsrop, cid=ad2ba9b4 is the current commit
220	   - @-, chid=wxtmosqz, cid=35ac2422 is the ancestor of the current commit
221	   - @--, chid=vktlnyvm, cid=a0b0285e is the ancestor of the ancestor and so on
222	
223	```
224	$ jj log -r 'all()'
225	@  tkpvsrop wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 ad2ba9b4
226	│  count 3
227	○  wxtmosqz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 35ac2422
228	│  count 2
229	○  vktlnyvm wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 a0b0285e
230	│  count 1
231	○  nvwytkrw wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 base 54fd18f7
232	│  count 0
233	◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
234	```
235	
236	Move @ to vktlnyvm. (This could also have been done with `jj edit -r @--`.)
237	
238	```
239	$ jj edit -r vkt
240	Working copy  (@) now at: vktlnyvm a0b0285e count 1
241	Parent commit (@-)      : nvwytkrw 54fd18f7 base | count 0
242	```
243	
244	### Referencing commits with relative revsets
245	
246	```
247	$ jj log -r @
248	@  vktlnyvm count 1
249	$ jj log -r @-
250	○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
251	$ jj log -r @--
252	◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
253	$ jj log -r @+
254	○  wxtmosqz count 2
255	$ jj log -r @++
256	○  tkpvsrop count 3
257	$ jj log -r @+++      # empty — nothing that far out
258	$ jj log -r @---      # empty
259	$ jj log -r @..
260	○  tkpvsrop count 3
261	○  wxtmosqz count 2
262	$ jj log -r @::
263	○  tkpvsrop count 3
264	○  wxtmosqz count 2
265	@  vktlnyvm count 1
266	$ jj log -r ..@
267	@  vktlnyvm count 1
268	○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
269	$ jj log -r ::@
270	@  vktlnyvm count 1
271	○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
272	◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
273	```
274	
275	#### Interpretation
276	
277	- `@-` and `@--` resolve to single revisions: parent and
278	  grandparent of @. `@+` and `@++` are children. The blank output
279	  for `@+++` and `@---` is the empty revset — there is no revision
280	  that far away in this chain.
281	- `@..` and `@::` are ranges going outward from @ toward
282	  descendants:
283	  - `@..` is the *open* form: descendants of @, **excluding** @
284	    itself (here: count 2 and count 3).
285	  - `@::` is the *closed* form: descendants of @, **including** @
286	    itself (here: @=count 1, count 2, count 3).
287	- `..@` and `::@` are ranges going inward from some implicit start
288	  toward ancestors of @:
289	  - `..@` includes @ and its ancestors but **excludes** the root
290	    commit (here: @=count 1, base=count 0).
291	  - `::@` includes the root commit as well.
292	
293	Mnemonic: `..` and `::` both produce ranges; `::` includes the
294	implicit endpoint (root or visible heads), `..` excludes it. The
295	named operand is always part of the result on the target side; on
296	the source side it depends on which dot-form is used (excluded by
297	`..`, included by `::`).
298	
299	### Referencing commits with absolute revsets
300	
301	```
302	$ jj log -r v          # unambiguous prefix of vktlnyvm
303	@  vktlnyvm count 1
304	$ jj log -r vktln
305	@  vktlnyvm count 1
306	$ jj log -r w+         # w matches wxtmosqz, + takes its child
307	○  tkpvsrop count 3
308	$ jj log -r w--
309	○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
310	$ jj log -r w..
311	○  tkpvsrop count 3
312	$ jj log -r ..w
313	○  wxtmosqz count 2
314	@  vktlnyvm count 1
315	○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
316	$ jj log -r w::
317	○  tkpvsrop count 3
318	○  wxtmosqz count 2
319	$ jj log -r ::w
320	○  wxtmosqz count 2
321	@  vktlnyvm count 1
322	○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
323	◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
324	```
325	
326	#### Interpretation
327	
328	- `v` and `vktln` both resolve to chid `vktlnyvm` because they are
329	  unambiguous prefixes within this repo — no other chid starts
330	  with those letters.
331	- `w+` resolves in two stages: `w` matches `wxtmosqz` (count 2) by
332	  prefix, then `+` takes its child, giving count 3.
333	- `w--` grandparent of w.
334	- `w..` descendants of w, excluding w.
335	- `..w` ancestors of w, not including root.
336	- `::w` ancestors of w, including root.
337	- `w::` descendants of w, including w.
338	
339	Prefix rule: jj rejects ambiguous prefixes. If two chids both
340	start with the same letters, the prefix must be lengthened until
341	exactly one chid matches.
342	
343	## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers
344	
345	Each commit cross-references its counterpart in the other repo
346	via an `ochid:` git trailer. That is a cross-repo convention
347	rather than a jj mechanic — for the full definition (trailer
348	syntax, per-commit mechanics, `.vc-config.toml`) see
349	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
350	in AGENTS.md.
351

# Jujutsu (jj) Tips

See [Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)
[Jujutsu-tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial)
and [jj docs](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/).

## Initial Commit for a repo

Create a directory and add your files.

Minimal commands to push

```
jj git init .
jj describe
jj git remote add origin git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
jj bookmark create main -r @
jj bookmark track main --remote=origin
jj git push
```

## Push a change to main

Assuming that this is to be push to main you
set the bookmark to the appropriate commit and
then just push:

```
jj bookmark set main -r @
jj git push
```

Complete example:
```

$ vi README.md

## Why `jj log` shows fewer commits than `gitk`

If you're coming from git, jj's log output can be surprising compared to
tools like `gitk --all`.

jj tracks *changes* (identified by change IDs), not individual git commits.
When you rewrite a change (`jj describe`, `jj rebase`, `jj squash`, etc.),
jj creates a new git commit and keeps the old one under `refs/jj/keep/*` as
undo history. `gitk --all` sees all of these obsolete commits; `jj log` only
shows the current version of each change.

## Useful commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `jj log` | Show recent visible commits (default revset) |
| `jj log -r ::@` | Show **all** ancestors of the working copy |
| `jj log -r 'all()'` | Show all non-hidden commits (needed if you have multiple heads/branches) |
| `jj st` | Show the status of the Working and Parent commits |
| `jj st -r <chid>` | Status of the commit, `<chid>` such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
| `jj show` | Show the Working commit (`-r @`) |
| `jj show -r <chid>` | Show the commit, `<chid>` such as `@`, `@-`, `xyz` |
| `jj evolog -r <chid>` | Show the evolution history of a single change |
| `jj op log` | Show operation history (each rewrite operation) |


In a single-branch workflow, `jj log -r ::@` and `jj log -r 'all()'` give
the same result. Use `all()` when you have multiple branches or heads.

## Revsets

Revsets (sets of revisions) are how jj addresses commits. A
revision is identified by `@`, a chid, or a cid, and revsets are
built from those identifiers plus the operators `-`, `+`, `..`,
and `::`. There is a complete language available — see
`jj help -k revsets`. The [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh)
script scaffolds the example repo used below.

Summary:

- A revision (rev) is used for addressing/referring to commits.
- jj supports a change_id (chid) and commit_id (cid).
  - The chid is permanent and doesn't change.
  - The cid is the SHA of the current commit and changes with any
    change to it or its ancestors.
- A revset is a set of revisions.
- In many jj commands revsets can address multiple commits.
- There is one root commit; chid=zzz sha=0 owner=root.
- The root commit never has content nor description.
- There can be multiple independent lineages of commits off root():
  - to create:
    - `jj new 'root()'`
    - `git switch --orphan <name>`
  - to see these:
    - `jj log -r 'roots(~root())'`
    - `git log --all --max-parents=0 --oneline`

The examples below show these primitives in action.

### A jj repo with some commits

Some examples of revisions/rev:
   - @, chid=tkpvsrop, cid=ad2ba9b4 is the current commit
   - @-, chid=wxtmosqz, cid=35ac2422 is the ancestor of the current commit
   - @--, chid=vktlnyvm, cid=a0b0285e is the ancestor of the ancestor and so on

```
$ jj log -r 'all()'
@  tkpvsrop wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 ad2ba9b4
│  count 3
○  wxtmosqz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 35ac2422
│  count 2
○  vktlnyvm wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 a0b0285e
│  count 1
○  nvwytkrw wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 base 54fd18f7
│  count 0
◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
```

Move @ to vktlnyvm. (This could also have been done with `jj edit -r @--`.)

```
$ jj edit -r vkt
Working copy  (@) now at: vktlnyvm a0b0285e count 1
Parent commit (@-)      : nvwytkrw 54fd18f7 base | count 0
```

### Referencing commits with relative revsets

```
$ jj log -r @
@  vktlnyvm count 1
$ jj log -r @-
○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
$ jj log -r @--
◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
$ jj log -r @+
○  wxtmosqz count 2
$ jj log -r @++
○  tkpvsrop count 3
$ jj log -r @+++      # empty — nothing that far out
$ jj log -r @---      # empty
$ jj log -r @..
○  tkpvsrop count 3
○  wxtmosqz count 2
$ jj log -r @::
○  tkpvsrop count 3
○  wxtmosqz count 2
@  vktlnyvm count 1
$ jj log -r ..@
@  vktlnyvm count 1
○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
$ jj log -r ::@
@  vktlnyvm count 1
○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
```

#### Interpretation

- `@-` and `@--` resolve to single revisions: parent and
  grandparent of @. `@+` and `@++` are children. The blank output
  for `@+++` and `@---` is the empty revset — there is no revision
  that far away in this chain.
- `@..` and `@::` are ranges going outward from @ toward
  descendants:
  - `@..` is the *open* form: descendants of @, **excluding** @
    itself (here: count 2 and count 3).
  - `@::` is the *closed* form: descendants of @, **including** @
    itself (here: @=count 1, count 2, count 3).
- `..@` and `::@` are ranges going inward from some implicit start
  toward ancestors of @:
  - `..@` includes @ and its ancestors but **excludes** the root
    commit (here: @=count 1, base=count 0).
  - `::@` includes the root commit as well.

Mnemonic: `..` and `::` both produce ranges; `::` includes the
implicit endpoint (root or visible heads), `..` excludes it. The
named operand is always part of the result on the target side; on
the source side it depends on which dot-form is used (excluded by
`..`, included by `::`).

### Referencing commits with absolute revsets

```
$ jj log -r v          # unambiguous prefix of vktlnyvm
@  vktlnyvm count 1
$ jj log -r vktln
@  vktlnyvm count 1
$ jj log -r w+         # w matches wxtmosqz, + takes its child
○  tkpvsrop count 3
$ jj log -r w--
○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
$ jj log -r w..
○  tkpvsrop count 3
$ jj log -r ..w
○  wxtmosqz count 2
@  vktlnyvm count 1
○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
$ jj log -r w::
○  tkpvsrop count 3
○  wxtmosqz count 2
$ jj log -r ::w
○  wxtmosqz count 2
@  vktlnyvm count 1
○  nvwytkrw base | count 0
◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
```

#### Interpretation

- `v` and `vktln` both resolve to chid `vktlnyvm` because they are
  unambiguous prefixes within this repo — no other chid starts
  with those letters.
- `w+` resolves in two stages: `w` matches `wxtmosqz` (count 2) by
  prefix, then `+` takes its child, giving count 3.
- `w--` grandparent of w.
- `w..` descendants of w, excluding w.
- `..w` ancestors of w, not including root.
- `::w` ancestors of w, including root.
- `w::` descendants of w, including w.

Prefix rule: jj rejects ambiguous prefixes. If two chids both
start with the same letters, the prefix must be lengthened until
exactly one chid matches.

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Each commit cross-references its counterpart in the other repo

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Build a 4-revision substep ladder for manual squash inspection.
#
# State after this script:
#   bookmark "base" -> empty commit (no counter.txt)
#   r1   counter.txt = "line 1 count 0"
#   r2   counter.txt = "line 1 count 1"
#   r3   counter.txt = "line 1 count 2"
#   r4   counter.txt = "line 1 count 3"   (@ sits here)
#
# Then run squash recipes by hand from /tmp/substep-test, e.g.:
#
#   jj squash --from "base..@-" --into @                            # downward
#
#   jj squash --from "base..@" --into base --ignore-immutable       # upward
#   jj edit @-

set -euo pipefail

ROOT=/tmp/substep-test

rm -rf "$ROOT"
mkdir -p "$ROOT"
cd "$ROOT"

jj git init --colocate >/dev/null

#jj describe -m "base (empty)" >/dev/null
jj bookmark create base -r @
#jj new >/dev/null

echo "count 0" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "count 0" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "count 1" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "count 1" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "count 2" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "count 2" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "count 3" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "count 3" >/dev/null

echo
echo "::: cwd=$ROOT"
echo
jj log -r "all()"
echo "----- counter.txt -----"
cat counter.txt

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
is the active one in this template), and earlier ones are closed and
kept for history.


Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):

- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
  Done entries.
- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
  rules, `Commits:` line format.
- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.

Per-cycle workflow lives in
[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
  sub-cycles.
- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
  work + commit description review gates.
- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix; body shape
  per app vs `.claude` repo.
- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.

This repo's versioning lives in
[`versioning.md`](versioning.md) — generic and shared
verbatim; the single source of truth that AGENTS.md and
cycle-protocol.md refer to abstractly:

- [Terms](versioning.md#terms) — version / version-of-record /
  versioning.
- [Recording the version-of-record](versioning.md#recording-the-version-of-record)
  — manifest, notation, reporter, and cadence, by medium.
- [Step numbering](versioning.md#step-numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` scheme, nesting, optional Preparation.

551	For each Work commit in the ladder:

572	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
573	  isolation.
574

576	descendants auto-rebase.

584	```

586	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`

589	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
   suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
   into one final commit.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and are
**collected under the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. While the ladder is being built
its commits are provisional — working titles, rewritten
in place — and serve mainly to sub-divide the work into
steps you can review in isolation. Whether they are
squashed into one commit or pushed as-is is decided at
push time (see [Pushing](#pushing)).

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
   suite, by which point it's harder to tell which step
   introduced them.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only; the sub-cycle Close-out collects everything
   into one final commit.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; handy for tracking down which
  step caused a problem.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

"working title **only**" and "collects everything into one final commit" both assume the squash happens. But the intro you just settled says squash-or-push-as-is is "decided at push time." If the ladder is pushed as-is, those working titles *become* the published titles — so they're not throwaway, and they'd want polishing. The squash recipe is the non-trivial part so it earns the detail — but there's no one-line "to push as-is, skip the squash and push the chain" counterpart.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.

intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

any other commit.

message.content: I'd like to make things simpler, if practical. Ideally we describe a "ladder" is a series of steps. Any given step in the ladder can be divide into a new set of steps and this is recursive. The way to "return" from the recursion is "close-out" the step series. Does that make sense and could we make is simpler if we explained it that way?

content.text:
Yes — that framing makes sense and it's accurate to the protocol. The mapping is clean:

- **ladder** = a cycle's Work-commit series
- **step** = one Work commit
- **subdivide a step** = a sub-cycle (the numbering already nests recursively: `-3` → `-3.1`, `-3.2`)
- **close-out** = the "return" — collect the sub-ladder's steps back under one Close-out, then the enclosing ladder continues

It's actually a *better* description than "when a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle…" because it names the recursion explicitly instead of describing one level of it. Two constraints I'll respect so it stays connected to the rest of the doc:

1. Keep the **`## Sub-cycle ladders`** heading text verbatim — its `#sub-cycle-ladders` anchor is linked from three other places in this file. (The recursive model lives in the body, not the title.)
2. Tie "step" to "Work commit" once, so this section doesn't fork a parallel vocabulary from the cycle/Work/Close-out terms used elsewhere.

Here's the simpler intro, applied:

in place — and serve mainly to sub-divide the work into
steps you can review in isolation. Whether they are

push time (see [Pushing](#pushing)).

## Sub-cycle ladders

A **ladder** is a series of steps — a cycle's Work
commits, built as a local jj `@` chain. The structure is
recursive:

- A **step** is one Work commit.
- Any step can subdivide into its **own ladder** of steps
  (a sub-cycle). The version suffix nests to match —
  `…-3`, then `…-3.1`, `…-3.2`; see [Numbering](#numbering).
- You **return** from a subdivision by **closing out**
  that ladder: its steps are collected back under one
  Close-out, then the enclosing ladder continues.

While a ladder is being built its steps are provisional —
working titles, rewritten in place — there to sub-divide
the work into pieces you can review in isolation. At each
close-out you decide whether to squash the steps into one
commit or push them as-is (see [Pushing](#pushing)).

### Per-step contract

For each step in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the step's work.
3. Run the fast validation (Rust example: `cargo test
   --bins`). **Non-negotiable** — for code, build and clippy
   alone miss regressions until a later commit runs the full
   suite, by which point it's harder to tell which step
   introduced them.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — a provisional
   title: the final description supersedes it if the
   ladder is squashed at Close-out, but it stands if the
   ladder is pushed unsquashed — so keep it presentable.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; handy for tracking down which
  step caused a problem.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash or push as-is

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass,
decide whether to squash (per [Pushing](#pushing)).

To **squash** into one commit:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

To **push as-is**, skip the squash and leave the chain
intact — each ladder commit ships as its own commit, so
give every one a presentable description first.

Either way, finish with `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for
any other commit.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same

content.text:

- **Intro** states it directly: a ladder is a series of steps; any step subdivides into its own ladder (sub-cycle), suffix nesting to match; you *return* by closing out that ladder. The squash-or-push-as-is decision rides along at each close-out.
- **Per-step contract** (renamed from "Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder") now uses "step" throughout.
- Navigating / Close-out / Recovery were already accurate and didn't need touching.

This is meaningfully simpler than the old "when a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle…" opener — it names the recursion once instead of narrating a single level, and the H2 anchor is preserved so the three inbound links still resolve.

One deliberate non-change: the later subsections still say "commit" in the mechanical spots ("all ladder **commits** are done", "review one **commit** in isolation", "any ladder **commit** rewrite it in place"). Those are literal jj-object operations, so I kept "commit" there and reserved "step" for the conceptual unit — the intro's "a step is one Work commit" bridges the two. If you'd rather see "step" everywhere for uniformity, say so and I'll harmonize the remaining handful.

content.text:
Not foolish at all — the recursion is genuinely there, and you've already half-exposed it with the new intro. Here's my honest read on how far it collapses and where it resists, so you can judge the payoff before touching anything.

## What collapses cleanly

**"Sub-cycle" can probably disappear as a term.** A sub-cycle *is* "a step that expanded into its own ladder." Nothing more. The nested numbering (`-3` → `-3.1`, `-3.2`) is just "the steps of the ladder that step 3 became." Your new intro already says almost exactly this — so "sub-cycle" is now mostly redundant restatement of the recursion. Dropping it is the high-payoff, low-risk move.

And yes — define **"a ladder is a series of steps" once at the top**, then let every later mention lean on it. That inverts the current state (defined at line 542, used from line 27).

## What resists collapse — two things the recursion can't absorb

1. **Phase roles are load-bearing for versioning.** Preparation / Work / Close-out aren't just narrative — they map to the version suffixes `-0` / `-N` / bare, which `versioning.md` and the `vc-x1` tooling parse. So even fully recursive, you still must say *which* step is Preparation and *which* is Close-out. The clean framing: "a ladder's optional first step is Preparation (`-0`), its last is Close-out (bare), the middle are Work (`-1`, `-2`…)" — that's a ladder's *anatomy*, stated once and applied at every depth. The roles survive; the word "sub-cycle" doesn't need to.

2. **Bookkeeping has an altitude the recursion doesn't.** This is the trap. Picking up a `## Todo` item, moving it to `## Done`, bumping the version-of-record, the mandatory publish — those are **top-level cycle** concerns. A sub-ladder doesn't pick up a new todo or write to `## Done`. The current doc already strains here: it claims "the same three-phase shape applies recursively at every depth," but the Preparation/Close-out *bookkeeping* really only happens at the top. If you collapse too hard, you'd imply every nested ladder does todo-pickup and Done-moves, which is false. So the simplification has to split **structural recursion (universal)** from **publishing/bookkeeping (top-level only)** — and that split is itself useful clarity.

## Blast radius — argues for staging

- **"sub-cycle"**: local, appears in a handful of spots, mostly in this one doc. Cheap to remove.
- **"cycle"**: everywhere — the doc's *title* (`cycle-protocol.md`, "Cycle Protocol"), `AGENTS.md`, `versioning.md`, `todo.md`, the chores conventions, and `vc-x1`'s vocabulary. Killing it is a big, cross-file rename.

So I'd recommend: **keep "cycle" as the name for the top-level published ladder** (the thing that carries a version and gets pushed), and **retire "sub-cycle"** in favor of "a step that subdivides into its own ladder." You get most of the verbage reduction for a fraction of the risk.

## One bonus you surfaced: "cycle" is overloaded

Your phrasing — "cycles… iterative development, make changes, review/test, before committing" — is a *third* sense of "cycle," distinct from the formal Preparation→Close-out unit. The doc also has the per-commit validation loop (the old "cargo cycle," now medium-neutral). So "cycle" currently means three things: the published unit, the per-commit edit/test loop, and the informal dev loop. ## Proposed close-out (fast)

**1. Capture the deferred rewrite** so it survives the session — one entry in `notes/todo-backlog.md` (durable, committed; per AGENTS.md we don't rely on memory). Proposed text:

> - Rename the protocol from "cycle" to **dev-protocol** and collapse the vocabulary onto the ladder recursion. "cycle" implies a loop; a ladder is linear. "sub-cycle" is just a step that expands into its own ladder. Pervasive rename (doc title, `AGENTS.md`, `versioning.md`, `todo.md`, `vc-x1`) — deferred. Key moves: define "a ladder is a series of steps" once at the top; drop "sub-cycle"; keep the phase roles (Preparation `-0` / Work `-N` / Close-out bare) as a ladder's *anatomy*; split structural recursion (universal) from publishing/bookkeeping (top-level only); disambiguate the overloaded "cycle" (published unit vs per-commit validation vs informal dev loop).

**3. Push** with your approval. Notes-only and there's no cargo preflight here, so `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --from message …` (skips preflight + the interactive review gate; we've already reviewed in-conversation).

The scaffold and our edits are one uncommitted blob in `@` — they'll land in the **same** commit. Good enough for a first commit, or do you want the bare scaffold committed separately first? My rec: one commit, keep it simple.
